behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is not only HMAC logic; it also requires verifying the reject path prevents queue handoff (HTTP handler + verifier + queue boundary). REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: no real OS/network/subprocess is required to catch this failure; an in-process HTTP handler test with a real queue adapter fake/spied module is sufficient. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: System was considered but rejected because behavior text only requires `401` and `not forwarded`, not real network conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Start the handler with real signature-verification middleware and a wired queue module in-process. Send a request with mismatched `X-Signature-256` and body. Assert HTTP 401 and assert zero enqueue writes/side effects.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly targets cross-module ordering/state handoff (`written to persistent queue before ... 200`). Unit is too low because single-function tests can miss ordering bugs across handler and queue adapter. REAL-INFRA: this can be validated with local persistence (e.g., sqlite/file-backed queue) without real external network. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior does not require real external HTTP/API/subprocess; ordering is observable in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler + real persistent queue module (local DB/filesystem). Instrument timestamps or transactional hooks so enqueue commit and response send are both observable. Assert enqueue commit happens before response completion.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is orchestration between request handler, enqueue, and downstream worker trigger. Unit is too low because this is about non-blocking interaction across modules. REAL-INFRA: no real OS/network/subprocess required; an in-process delayed downstream processor can expose blocking regressions. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior text is about waiting semantics, not real network/OS effects.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler to a downstream processor that intentionally delays completion. Send a valid webhook and assert response is returned before downstream promise resolves, while enqueue still occurred.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: this is an end-to-end latency SLA (`within 3 seconds wall-clock`) under concurrency; in-process mocks can hide event-loop starvation/socket backlog behavior. MOCK-HIDE: fake transport and synthetic timers may pass while real HTTP serving under concurrent load fails. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: Integration was considered but rejected due explicit `wall-clock` + `slow and concurrent` wording, which requires real runtime/HTTP behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run the service as a real process with its actual HTTP server. Preload slow concurrent downstream work, then send webhook requests via real HTTP client and measure end-to-end latency. Assert response deadline <= 3s while slow prior work is active.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: This is dedup state propagation across request handling and persistent queue/idempotency store, not isolated logic. Unit can miss race/order/state handoff defects. REAL-INFRA: local persistent store wiring is enough; no external OS/network primitives required beyond normal local DB/filesystem integration. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior text does not require real external network/subprocess behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Use real handler + real persistent dedup/queue storage. Submit the same event ID twice (including close timing). Assert only one queue write/record exists and second request does not create another enqueue.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is module interaction: queue-full signal must map to specific HTTP semantics (503 + `Retry-After`). Unit of either module alone is insufficient for mapping correctness. REAL-INFRA: this can be caught with in-process queue module (or controlled local queue state), no real external network needed. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior only specifies response mapping on queue status, not real OS/network conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler with queue implementation configured to return full-capacity status. Send valid request; assert status 503 and `Retry-After` header presence/value policy, and no successful enqueue.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: Behavior targets real queue module + persistence boundary (`persistent queue actually enforces`) and configuration/state interaction. Unit with mocked persistence can miss true enforcement defects. REAL-INFRA: local DB/filesystem-backed queue is sufficient and fits Integration; no external API/subprocess is required. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior does not require real external network/OS primitives beyond local persistence.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue backend with a small capacity. Fill to limit, then attempt one additional enqueue. Assert backend returns rejection/error and persisted depth never exceeds configured capacity.
